locked_facts: First law (Law of Orbits): Planets move in elliptical orbits with the Sun at one focus
Second law (Law of Areas): Line joining planet to Sun sweeps equal areas in equal time intervals
Third law (Law of Periods): T² ∝ a³, or T² = (4π²/GM)a³
Second law is a consequence of conservation of angular momentum
Planet moves fastest at perihelion (closest to Sun) and slowest at aphelion (farthest)
Third law applies to any two bodies orbiting the same central mass
truth_statements: Kepler's first law states that all planets orbit the Sun in ellipses with the Sun at one focus
Kepler's second law is equivalent to conservation of angular momentum — no external torque acts on the planet
Kepler's third law states that the square of the period is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis
A planet moves faster when it is closer to the Sun and slower when it is farther — this follows from the equal area law
The third law allows comparison of orbits: T₁²/T₂² = a₁³/a₂³ for any two planets

aha_moment.what_to_show: An elliptical orbit with area sectors marked at equal time intervals. Near perihelion: the sector is a thin, long triangle (fast planet). Near aphelion: the sector is a wide, short triangle. Both have the SAME area. The angular momentum L = mvr is constant — at smaller r, v must increase.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Colour-matched equal-area sectors at perihelion (thin) and aphelion (wide) with velocity arrows
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students memorise 'equal areas in equal times' without connecting it to speed variation — seeing the sector shapes makes the speed change obvious

simulation_states.label: Elliptical orbit
simulation_states.description: Planet orbiting Sun in an ellipse. Sun is at one focus (not the centre). Perihelion and aphelion marked. The orbit is clearly not a circle.
simulation_states.emphasis: The Sun is at a focus, not the centre — orbits are ellipses

simulation_states.label: Equal areas in equal times
simulation_states.description: Time-lapse showing area swept in fixed time intervals. Near perihelion: fast motion, thin sector. Near aphelion: slow motion, wide sector. Areas computed and shown to be equal.
simulation_states.emphasis: Equal areas ↔ angular momentum conservation ↔ faster at perihelion

simulation_states.label: Third law: T² ∝ a³
simulation_states.description: Multiple orbits with different semi-major axes. For each: T and a measured. T²/a³ computed — same for all orbits around the same Sun. Graph: T² vs a³ is a straight line.
simulation_states.emphasis: T²/a³ = constant for all planets orbiting the same star

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts orbital eccentricity and semi-major axis. Planet traces orbit with speed variation visible. Area sectors highlighted. T computed and compared against T² ∝ a³ prediction.
simulation_states.emphasis: Change eccentricity — see speed variation increase. Change a — see T change.

common_misconceptions.belief: The Sun is at the centre of the elliptical orbit
common_misconceptions.correction: The Sun is at one of the two foci of the ellipse, not at the centre. This is why the planet-Sun distance varies throughout the orbit.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Clearly mark both foci — Sun at one, the other empty. Show varying distance throughout orbit.

common_misconceptions.belief: Planets move at constant speed in their orbits
common_misconceptions.correction: Planets move faster when closer to the Sun (perihelion) and slower when farther (aphelion). This follows from Kepler's second law and conservation of angular momentum.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Velocity arrow length changes dramatically between perihelion and aphelion
